nativeRange.habitat: Seagrass beds and banks; occasionally found in bays; not commonly associated with coral reefs. Demersal species found at depths of roughly 10–19 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on fish, squid, octopus, and crustaceans. Newly acquired specimens have been known to initially prefer live feeder fish; with patience, many individuals can be transitioned to frozen meaty foods such as silversides, squid, and krill. Target feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is generally advisable to avoid accidental hand bites.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth

summary: The Black Edge Moray Eel (Gymnothorax saxicola) is a Western Atlantic moray from seagrass beds and coastal banks. Generally considered intermediate care; tends to be secretive and nocturnal, requiring ample rockwork for shelter and a tightly-sealed lid.
careNotes:
The Black Edge Moray Eel (also known as Honeycomb Moray or Ocellated Moray) is native to the Western Atlantic, ranging from New Jersey and Bermuda south to southern Florida and the eastern Gulf of Mexico, with records from Cuba and Belize. In the wild, it is most commonly associated with seagrass beds and shallow coastal banks (10–19 m depth); it is generally not found on coral reefs, though it adapts well to reef aquarium rockwork in captivity.

Escape risk: Like all moray eels, this species is a determined escape artist with a surprisingly powerful body. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is considered essential — any gap large enough for the eel's body is a potential exit point. Escape risk is high.

Nocturnal behavior: This species tends to be crepuscular to nocturnal. Newly acquired specimens typically remain hidden in rock crevices with their mouth visibly open (a normal behavior, not aggression). Over time, many individuals become bolder and begin displaying openly during the day.

Feeding: Initially may prefer live feeder fish; patient transition to frozen meaty foods (squid, silversides, krill) is generally achievable. Target feeding with tongs reduces the risk of accidental hand bites. This eel has been documented to eat fish and invertebrates that fit in its mouth — ornamental shrimp and small tankmates are at risk.

Reef compatibility: Rated 'with caution' because it tends to leave corals and sessile invertebrates undisturbed, but will readily consume fish and mobile invertebrates (shrimp, crabs) that fit in its mouth.

Temperature cross-source note: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase documents a preferred range of 23.4–28°C. Averaged values of 22.8–26.8°C are used here. Both sources are consistent in the overlap zone (23–26°C).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (60 cm TL, consistent with LiveAquaria's 24 in / 60.96 cm), native range (Western Atlantic, New Jersey to Gulf of Mexico, Cuba, Belize), habitat (seagrass beds, not coral reefs), taxonomy (Muraenidae / Anguilliformes), and temperature (23.4–28°C — wider range than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F). Temperature values averaged per playbook. FishBase common name is 'Honeycomb moray'; 'Black Edge Moray' appears to be a LiveAquaria trade name. FishBase does not list 'Black Edge Moray' in its common-names registry.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous in the wild (broadcast egg-laying); captive breeding has not been documented for this species.
